lesson:
始める is transitive — you start something. Its intransitive partner is 始まる (はじまる) — something starts on its own. 仕事を始める (I start work). 映画が始まる (the movie starts). This is a classic える/ある pair, and the pattern repeats: 終える/終わる, 変える/変わる, 決める/決まる. Internalize this one pair and the others click into place.

Key pattern: 〜始める attached to a verb’s masu-stem means “start doing.” 雨が降り始めた (it started raining). 読み始める (start reading). The mirror of 〜終わる. Together they bracket any action.

The kanji 始 combines 女 (woman) + 台 (platform). The connection traces to the idea of a woman as the origin of life — birth as the first beginning. It appears in 原始 (げんし, primitive) and 始祖 (しそ, founder).

Particles: 始める takes を — the thing you start is your direct object. 新しい生活を始める (start a new life).

Potential が始められる: が marks what can be started. 来月から授業が始められる (classes can be started from next month).

Passive に始められる: に marks who started it. 先に始められた (they started before me).

Causative に始めさせる: に marks who you make start. 生徒に練習を始めさせる (have the students start practice).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 始まる (はじまる)
Dictionaryはじめるを
Negativeはじめないを
Politeはじめますを
Polite negはじめませんを
Pastはじめたを
Polite pastはじめましたを
て-formはじめてを
Volitionalはじめようを
Polite volはじめましょうを
Passiveはじめられるに
Causativeはじめさせるに／を
Caus-passはじめさせられるに

Imperativeはじめろを
Imp なさいはじめなさいを
Cond ～ばはじめればを
Cond ～たらはじめたらを

meaning: to wake, get up; to occur
lesson:
起きる has two lives: people wake up, and events happen. 朝早く起きる (wake up early). 事故が起きた (an accident occurred). The connection is vertical motion — your body rises from sleep, a situation rises into being. The subject tells you which meaning: a person wakes up, a phenomenon arises.

Three related verbs: 起きる (intransitive — wake up, occur). 起こる (おこる) (also intransitive but only “occur”). 起こす (おこす) (transitive — wake someone up, cause something). 子供を起こす (wake the kid up). 問題を起こす (cause a problem). For “wake someone up,” speakers usually reach for 起こす rather than the causative of 起きる.

The kanji 起: 走 (run) + 己 (self). The self getting up and moving.

Particles: 起きる takes が for the subject — the person who wakes or the event that occurs. 子供が起きた (the kid woke up). No direct object — it's intransitive. For time, use に: 六時に起きる (wake up at six).

Passive: not commonly used — waking up is something you do, not something done to you. For events, you'd say 起こされた (the transitive causative) if someone caused it.

Causative を／に起きさせる: making someone wake up. 子供を起きさせる (make the kid get up). In practice, 起こす (the transitive pair) is more natural for "wake someone up."

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 起こす (おこす)

In Japan, new beginnings don’t cluster around January. They cluster around April — the start of the school year, the fiscal year, new jobs, fresh starts. And April is 春. Cherry blossoms, graduation ceremonies, first days at work. 春休み (はるやすみ, spring break) isn’t a vacation in the middle of the year — it’s the transition between endings and new beginnings.

The word はる may be related to 張る (はる, to swell/stretch) — the season when buds swell and burst open. Some linguists connect it to 墾る (はる, to plow), since spring is when farmers begin working fields. Either etymology roots the word in physical expansion — things opening up after winter’s constriction.

The kanji 春 has 日 (sun) at the bottom and strokes on top that originally represented emerging plants. Sun warming the earth, making things push upward.

午前 is the mirror of 午後: 午 (noon) + 前 (before) = "before noon." Together with 午後, they split the day at its midpoint. Clean, symmetrical, borrowed wholesale from the Chinese system of dividing time by the zodiac hours.

In usage, 午前 goes before the time: 午前十時 (10 AM). But here's something worth noticing: Japanese people don't always say 午前 for "morning" in casual speech. They're more likely to say 朝 (あさ) for early morning or just use context. 午前 is the precise, clock-oriented word — schedules, appointments, news broadcasts. 朝 is the lived experience of morning — waking up, the commute, breakfast.

午前中 (ごぜんちゅう) means "during the morning" or "throughout the morning" and is extremely useful: 午前中に届きます (it'll arrive sometime in the morning). The 中 adds a sense of "somewhere within that span" — no exact time, just the general morning window.

別に。 One word. Delivered with a shrug. It means “not particularly,” “I don’t really care,” or “nothing special.” In anime and drama, 別に... followed by a trailing-off is the classic tsundere response — pretending not to care while clearly caring. In real life: 別にいい (it’s fine, whatever). 別に何もない (nothing in particular).

The core adjective means “separate, different.” As a な-adjective: 別な問題 or 別の話 (a different topic). Both 別な and 別の are correct — 別の is slightly more common. The kanji 別 has a blade (刂) on the right — cutting apart. It shows up in 別れる (わかれる, to part), 区別 (くべつ, distinction), 特別 (とくべつ, special).

Dictionaryべつだ
Negativeべつじゃない
Pastべつだった
Past negべつじゃなかった
Adverbべつに
て-formべつで

どこ is the question member of the ko-so-a-do place series: ここ (here), そこ (there, near you), あそこ (over there), どこ (where?). The system is perfectly regular — swap the first syllable and the meaning changes. You already know the logic from これ/それ/あれ/どれ.

どこに行くの？ (where are you going?). トイレはどこですか (where's the bathroom?). どこから来ましたか (where are you from?). The particle after どこ shapes the question: に asks where you're heading, で asks where something happens, から asks where something comes from.

Like all question words in Japanese, どこ shapeshifts with particles: どこか (somewhere), どこも (everywhere / nowhere, depending on positive or negative), どこでも (anywhere). The pattern is identical to いつか/いつも/いつでも — learn one question word's particle combinations and you've learned them all.

The reading is へや. Not ぶや, not べや — へや. Neither kanji’s standard reading produces this sound. Just memorize it as a unit. 部 (section) + 屋 (roof, building). A sectioned-off space under a roof.

屋 is worth knowing on its own. It turns any product into the shop that sells it: 本屋 (ほんや, bookstore), 花屋 (はなや, florist), パン屋 (bakery). Learn a product noun, slap 屋 on the end, get the store.

部屋 is the default word for any room. 部屋に入る (enter a room). 部屋を片付ける (tidy up). In sumo, 部屋 also means a wrestling stable — 相撲部屋 (すもうべや) — where wrestlers live and train together.

若者 (わかもの, young people) shows up in news articles whenever a journalist wants to discuss generational trends. 若手 (わかて) is the workplace word for someone junior, still rising. Both treat youth not as an age but as a stage — not yet fully developed, still proving yourself. That’s the core feeling of 若い: fresh, unfinished, with growth ahead.

若い usually describes people but stretches to anything fresh or unripe. 若い人 (a young person). 若い頃 (when I was young). 若く見える (look young) — a compliment in any culture, but especially effective in Japan.

The kanji 若 has the grass radical (艸) on top — young shoots, tender growth. In Buddhist terminology, 般若 (はんにゃ) uses 若 for a completely different meaning (wisdom) — a reminder that many kanji live entirely different lives in different contexts.

Dictionaryわかい
Negativeわかくない
Pastわかかった
Past negわかくなかった
Adverbわかく
て-formわかくて

The word くるま likely comes from くる (to spin) + ま (round thing) — the spinning round thing. A wheel. Then the thing with wheels. Then the car. It’s a rare case where you can trace a modern Japanese word back to its physical origin and see the logic at every step.

The kanji 車 matches: a top-down view of a cart with the axle running through the middle, two wheels on either side. Oracle bone versions from 3,500 years ago show a two-wheeled chariot so clearly you could build one from the drawing.

In modern Japanese, 車 defaults to “car.” 車で行く (go by car). 車を運転する (drive). But the older meaning survives in compounds: 電車 (でんしゃ, train), 自転車 (じてんしゃ, bicycle — “self-rotating vehicle”), 風車 (ふうしゃ, windmill).

〜ておく (often contracted to 〜とく in casual speech) means to do something in advance. 買っておく (buy ahead of time). 調べておく (look it up beforehand). そのままにしておく (leave it as it is). The image: placing an action down for later use. This grammar pattern alone makes 置く one of the most important verbs you’ll learn.

On its own, 置く means to put or place something deliberately. テーブルに置く (put on the table). 鍵をどこに置いた？ (where did I put the keys?). The particle に marks location, を marks the thing. The kanji 置 has 网 (net) over 直 (straight) — setting something down in a fixed position.

Particles: 置く takes を for the thing placed and に for the location. 荷物をここに置く (put the luggage here).

Potential が置ける: が marks what can be placed. ここに車が置ける (you can park a car here).

Passive に置かれる: に marks who placed it. 変な場所に置かれた (it was placed in a weird spot).

Causative に…を置かせる: に marks who you make put something down. 子供に荷物を置かせる (have the kid put down the bags).

You’ll almost never use 住む in its dictionary form. Once you live somewhere, you’re in the state of living there. So Japanese uses 住んでいる: 東京に住んでいる (I live in Tokyo). This mirrors 知る — the dictionary form describes the moment of transition, the ている form describes the resulting state. If someone asks where you live, the answer is always 〜に住んでいます.

The kanji 住 is 亻 (person) + 主 (master). A person who is the master of a place — settled, rooted. 住む is about where your life is anchored. Not being alive (生きる), not temporarily staying (泊まる).

住 feeds into useful compounds: 住所 (じゅうしょ, address), 住民 (じゅうみん, resident), 住宅 (じゅうたく, housing). The on’yomi じゅう appears in formal contexts. The kun’yomi すむ stays in everyday speech.

Particles: 住む takes に — the place where you live. 日本に住む (live in Japan). に marks the location of residence, the place you're rooted to.

Passive に住まれる: uncommon, but possible in indirect passive — 隣に変な人に住まれて困っている (a weird person lives next door and it's a problem for me).

Causative を／に住ませる: making or letting someone live somewhere. に for permissive — 学生にこのアパートに住ませる (let students live in this apartment).

働 is rare among kanji — it was invented in Japan, not imported from China. It's a 国字 (こくじ, national character), combining 亻 (person) + 動 (move). A person in motion. That's the Japanese definition of work: a human being moving, doing, physically engaging with the world. The Chinese character for work (工) is abstract — a beam or tool. The Japanese invention is embodied — it's about the worker, not the work.

働く is intransitive — you work, but you don't "work" an object. The place where you work takes で: 会社で働く (work at a company). The field takes で too: IT業界で働いている (I work in the IT industry). Like 住む, you'll most often use the ている form to describe your ongoing work situation.

働く can also mean "to function" or "to operate" for non-humans: 頭が働かない (my brain isn't working). 薬が働く (the medicine takes effect). And in a darker sense: 悪事を働く (commit a crime) — here 働く unusually takes を, treating the wrongdoing as the object of labor.

Particles: 働く takes で for the workplace — 病院で働く (work at a hospital). で marks where the action happens. For the company or organization specifically, に also works: トヨタに働いている.

Passive に働かれる: indirect passive — someone working affects you. Rare in natural speech.

Causative を／に働かせる: making someone work. を for coercive — 社員を休みなく働かせる (make employees work without days off). A word you see in discussions about labor rights.

難しい顔をする — “to make a difficult face.” It means to look troubled or stern, and it reveals something about the word: むずかしい isn’t just about hard math. It can describe people who are hard to deal with — picky, demanding, socially complicated. あの人は難しい means “that person is difficult” the way English might say “high-maintenance.”

For its primary meaning, 難しい describes intellectual or situational complexity. 日本語は難しい (Japanese is hard). It’s about things that challenge understanding, not things that exhaust you — for that, use 大変 (たいへん). A puzzle is 難しい. Moving house while working full-time is 大変. One challenges your brain. The other exhausts your everything.

The kanji 難 originally meant hardship or disaster. 隹 (short-tailed bird) plus a complex left component. The character still carries both senses: 災難 (さいなん, disaster). Difficulty and suffering share a kanji.

Dictionaryむずかしい
Negativeむずかしくない
Pastむずかしかった
Past negむずかしくなかった
Adverbむずかしく
て-formむずかしくて

Your doctor is a 先生. Your lawyer is a 先生. The manga artist whose work you follow is a 先生. Politicians get called 先生. The word extends far beyond classroom teachers — it honors anyone with knowledge or authority that came from getting there first. The literal meaning tells you why: 先 (before, ahead) + 生 (born, life) = someone born before you, someone who arrived first.

In practice, 先生 functions both as a noun (“a teacher”) and as a title. When used as a title, it replaces さん — you don’t say 田中先生さん. And you can address someone directly as just 先生, the way English uses “Doctor” or “Professor” standalone.

There’s an ironic usage too. Sarcastically calling someone 先生 implies they’re being arrogant or self-important. The same word that conveys deep respect can, with the right tone, convey the opposite.

役に立つ (to be useful — to stand in service). 腹が立つ (to get angry — your belly rises). 目立つ (to stand out — eyes drawn to it). 立場 (たちば, standpoint). The vertical image runs through all of them — being upright means being visible, active, present. 立つ has one of the richest idiomatic lives of any Japanese verb.

The verb itself is intransitive. ここに立って (stand here). 椅子から立つ (stand up from a chair). Its transitive partner is 立てる (たてる) — to set something upright, to establish: 計画を立てる (make a plan). 旗を立てる (raise a flag).

The kanji 立 is a person on the ground. Two legs on a horizontal line. This picture has been stable for over three thousand years.

Particles: 立つ takes に for where you stand — 入口に立つ (stand at the entrance). For rising from somewhere, から: 席から立つ (stand up from your seat).

Passive に立たれる: indirect passive — someone standing somewhere affects you. 前に立たれて見えない (someone stood in front of me and I can't see).

Causative を／に立たせる: making someone stand. を for coercive — 生徒を廊下に立たせる (make a student stand in the hallway — a classic Japanese school punishment).

パーティーに呼ぶ means “invite to a party.” 呼ぶ means “to call,” so why does it also mean “invite”? Because calling someone to a place implies you want them there. The word doesn’t draw a hard line between summoning and welcoming — whether it’s a command or a warm invitation depends on relationship and context.

呼ぶ covers calling out to someone and calling someone something. 名前を呼ぶ (call someone’s name). タクシーを呼ぶ (call a taxi). 彼を「先生」と呼ぶ (call him “sensei”). The particle と frames the name, just like with 言う.

The kanji 呼 has 口 (mouth) and 乎 (an exclamation). A mouth crying out. The original sense was exhaling forcefully. It’s the opposite of 吸 (すう, inhale), and together they form 呼吸 (こきゅう, breathing).

Particles: 呼ぶ takes を for the person called and と for what you call them. 彼を「田中さん」と呼ぶ (call him "Tanaka-san").

Potential が呼べる: が marks who can be called. ここからタクシーが呼べる (you can call a taxi from here).

Passive に呼ばれる: に marks who calls you. 先生に呼ばれた (I was called by the teacher). Very common — being summoned is a frequent experience.

Causative に…を呼ばせる: に marks who you make do the calling. 子供に友達を呼ばせる (have the kid call their friends).

In Japan, which 大学 you attend often matters more for your career than what you study there. 東大 (とうだい, University of Tokyo), 京大 (きょうだい, Kyoto University), 早稲田, 慶応 — these names carry weight in job interviews decades after graduation. 大学に入る (enter university) is one of the defining moments of a Japanese person’s life, preceded by the infamous 受験 (じゅけん, entrance exam) period that consumes the final year of high school.

The kanji: 大 (big) + 学 (study) = big study, grand learning. In the Confucian tradition, 大学 was one of the Four Books — a foundational text about self-cultivation. The word carried philosophical weight long before it meant an institution with a campus and tuition.

Related words: 大学生 (だいがくせい, university student). 大学院 (だいがくいん, graduate school). 短大 (たんだい, junior college — short for 短期大学). The abbreviation pattern is common: long compound → first kanji of each part.

〜やすい attached to a verb’s masu-stem means “easy to do” — one of the most useful suffixes in the language. 読みやすい (easy to read). 分かりやすい (easy to understand). 壊れやすい (fragile). This suffix is technically a different word from the adjective 安い, written in hiragana. But the sound-meaning bridge is real: easy, not burdensome. Learn the suffix here and you’ll use it for everything.

The adjective 安い itself means “cheap.” この店は安い. 安くて美味しい (cheap and delicious). It’s the opposite of 高い in the price sense.

But the kanji 安 tells a different story. A woman (女) under a roof (宀). The original meaning was “peace, safety.” That older sense lives on: 安心 (あんしん, peace of mind), 安全 (あんぜん, safety), 不安 (ふあん, anxiety). When 安 is read あん in compounds, it means safety. As やすい, it means cheap.

Dictionaryやすい
Negativeやすくない
Pastやすかった
Past negやすくなかった
Adverbやすく
て-formやすくて

もっと is the everyday, colloquial "more" — the one you reach for in conversation. もっと食べて (eat more). もっと大きい (bigger). もっと早く言って (you should have said so sooner — literally, say it more early). It intensifies whatever follows — more of the same quality, action, or degree.

Japanese doesn't have comparative adjective forms the way English does (no "-er" suffix). Instead, you just put もっと in front of the adjective: もっと安い (cheaper). もっと難しい (harder). Clean and consistent — no irregular comparatives, no "good/better/best" nightmares. Just もっと + any adjective.

もっと vs もう: もっと asks for a greater degree of the same thing. もう (+ a number/quantity) asks for an additional unit. もっと水 = more water (a larger amount). もう一杯 = another glass (one more of the same). The difference matters: もっと adjusts the dial. もう adds to the count.

帰りたい — “I want to go home.” Possibly the most relatable sentence in any language. In Japanese workplaces, お先に失礼します (excuse me for leaving before you) is the ritual phrase for going home, because leaving before colleagues requires an apology. 帰る is loaded with social weight in work culture.

帰る specifically means returning to where you belong. Home. Your country. Your base. This is the key distinction from 戻る (もどる): both mean “return,” but 帰る points homeward while 戻る means going back to any previous location. 家に帰る (go home). 席に戻る (return to your seat). You wouldn’t say 席に帰る because your seat isn’t home.

The kanji 帰: the left component relates to a marching army, the right (帚) is a broom. Soldiers returning home, brooms sweeping the path for their arrival.

Particles: 帰る takes に for the destination — 家に帰る (go home). It also takes から for where you're returning from: 学校から帰る (come home from school).

Passive に帰られる: indirect passive — someone going home affects you. 彼女に帰られた (she went home — and I was left behind / it affected me negatively). The に marks the person whose departure impacted you.

Causative を／に帰らせる: making or letting someone go home. を for coercive — 生徒を帰らせる (send the student home). に for permissive — 社員に早く帰らせる (let the employee go home early).

Causativeかえらせるを／に

Imp なさいかえりなさいに
Cond ～ばかえればに
Cond ～たらかえったらに

Most Japanese verbs let you be the one doing something. 分かる refuses. In English, "I understand the problem" puts you in the driver's seat. In Japanese, 問題が分かる — the problem is the subject. It's the thing that becomes clear. You're just standing there when clarity arrives. Understanding isn't an action. It's weather.

The kanji 分 is a knife (刀) splitting something in two (八). Dividing, distinguishing, telling apart. 分かる is the moment confusion cracks open and you can see through it.

分かる vs 知る: 知る is about possessing information — do you have this fact stored? 分かる is about comprehension — does this click? 住所を知っている (I know the address). この文法が分かる (I understand this grammar). You 知る a phone number. You 分かる calculus.

分かった is technically past tense, but in conversation it means "got it" — an acknowledgment, not a report about history. Someone explains, you say 分かった.

Particles: 分かる takes が — the thing understood is the grammatical subject. 日本語が分かる (Japanese is understandable to me). You aren't acting on it; it's revealing itself to you.

Passive: Rarely used — 分かる already works like an English passive. に分かられる exists in theory but is uncommon.

Causative を／に分からせる: making someone understand. に marks the person: 子供に分からせる (make the child understand).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryわかるが
Negativeわからないが

A 大きい room might have high ceilings — volume, overall bigness. A 広い room has floor space you can move in. That's the distinction. 大きい measures in three dimensions. 広い measures in two — spread, area, room to breathe. A warehouse is 大きい. A plain stretching to the horizon is 広い.

広い部屋. 広い道. 広い心 (a broad mind — generosity of spirit). The word doesn't care which direction the space extends. If there's room, it's 広い.

The kanji 広 has the shelter radical (广) — a roof with one side open, extending outward. The verbs 広がる (ひろがる, to spread out — intransitive) and 広げる (ひろげる, to spread something — transitive) grow from the same root and come up constantly: 草原が広がっている (the grassland stretches out). 地図を広げる (unfold a map).

Dictionaryひろい
Negativeひろくない
Pastひろかった
Past negひろくなかった
Adverbひろく
て-formひろくて

数を数える — かずをかぞえる — "count the count." Same kanji twice, different readings each time. Welcome to 数, a word with multiple lives.

As かず (native reading, standalone word): quantity. How many. 数が多い (there are many). 数が足りない (there aren't enough). As すう (Chinese-derived, in compounds): 数学 (すうがく, mathematics), 数字 (すうじ, numeral). And as a prefix, 数〜 read すう means "several" — 数日 (すうじつ, several days), 数回 (すうかい, several times). Vague, small, somewhere between a few and a handful.

The kanji packs in 米 (rice), 女 (woman), and 攵 (a hand with a stick). Someone tallying grains of rice. Counting as a physical, agricultural act — not abstract arithmetic.

この近くにコンビニある？ — probably the first sentence you'll actually need in Japan. It uses 近く, the adverb form of 近い, which pulls double duty as a noun meaning "the vicinity." 駅の近く (near the station, the area around it). Learn 近く as a word in its own right, not just a conjugation.

近い handles both physical and abstract proximity. 駅に近い (close to the station). 答えに近い (close to the answer). 近い将来 (the near future). The に marks what you're close to.

The kanji 近: road radical (辶) plus 斤 (an axe). Axe range — you have to be right next to something to swing one.

Dictionaryちかい
Negativeちかくない
Pastちかかった
Past negちかくなかった
Adverbちかく
て-formちかくて

そこ is "there" — but specifically there near you, the listener. Not far away (that's あそこ). Not here by me (that's ここ). The spot in your zone. English collapses the last two into one "there." Japanese insists on knowing whose territory the place belongs to.

そこにある (it's there, by you). そこに座って (sit there). In conversation, そこ also refers back to a place just mentioned: 昨日レストランに行った。そこで友達に会った。 (Went to a restaurant yesterday. Ran into a friend there.) The restaurant enters shared conversational space, so it becomes そこ.

Say ha-shi-ru fast a few times. Three sharp syllables that feel like feet hitting pavement. Now say a-ru-ku (to walk). Slower, plodding. The sounds match the speeds.

The kanji 走 was, in oracle bone script, a person running — full figure, arms swinging, legs mid-stride. Three thousand years of simplification compressed it, but the energy survived.

走る covers every kind of running. A person: 公園を走る. A car: 車が走る. Pain through your body: 痛みが走る. If something is covering ground rapidly, it's 走る.

Particles: 走る takes を to mark the path — 道を走る (run along the road). This is route-marking を, not direct object を. で marks the general location: 公園で走る.

Passive に走られる: indirect passive — someone running affects you. 犬に走られて追いつけなかった (the dog ran off and I couldn't catch up).

Causative を／に走らせる: を is coercive — 生徒を走らせる (make the students run). に is permissive.

Polite negはしりませんを
Pastはしったを
Polite pastはしりましたを
て-formはしってを
Volitionalはしろうを
Polite volはしりましょうを
Passiveはしられるに
Causativeはしらせるを／に
Caus-passはしらせられるに
Potentialはしれるを
Imperativeはしれを
Imp なさいはしりなさいを
Cond ～ばはしればを
Cond ～たらはしったらを

電気を入れる — turn on the power. お茶を入れる — brew tea. 力を入れる — put in effort. Three completely different English verbs, one Japanese verb. 入れる is "put in," and that phrase covers an astonishing range. Electricity into a circuit. Tea leaves into water. Energy into a task. Each time, something is being placed inside or activated through insertion.

The kanji 入: two strokes sliding downward into an opening. One of the most visually transparent characters in the language. 入れる is transitive — you do the putting. Its intransitive partner 入る (はいる) means something goes in by itself. Lock in the pair: コーヒーに砂糖を入れる (put sugar in coffee) vs 部屋に入る (enter a room).

Particles: 入れる takes を for what you put in, に for where. 箱にりんごを入れる (put apples in the box).

Potential が入れられる: が marks what can be put in. まだ荷物が入れられる (more luggage can still fit).

Passive に入れられる: に marks who did the putting. コーヒーに砂糖を入れられた (someone put sugar in my coffee — and I didn't want it).

Causative に…を入れさせる: に marks who you make do the inserting. 子供にお金を貯金箱に入れさせる (make the kid put money in the piggy bank).

電話番号を教えて doesn't mean "teach me your phone number." It means "tell me." That's the surprise: 教える covers both teaching (sustained knowledge transfer) and simply sharing information (a quick handoff). 英語を教える (teach English). 道を教える (tell someone the way). Japanese sees both as the same act — you have knowledge, you pass it on.

The kanji 教: 孝 (a child learning from an elder) plus 攵 (a hand with a rod). Teaching in its oldest form — an elder with a stick, guiding a younger person. The flow is always one-directional: from someone who knows to someone who doesn't.

Particles: 教える takes を for what you teach and に for who. 生徒に数学を教える (teach students math). The person receiving knowledge takes に — they're the destination.

Potential が教えられる: が marks the subject that can be taught. この科目が教えられる (this subject can be taught).

Passive に教えられる: に marks the teacher. 先生に大事なことを教えられた (I was taught something important by the teacher).

Causative に教えさせる: に marks who you make teach. 先輩に新人を教えさせる (have the senior teach the newcomer).

A-ru-ku. Three even syllables, each one a footfall. Step. Step. Step. Compare with 走る (はしる) — sharper, faster. The sounds match the paces.

The kanji 歩 was originally two footprints alternating — left foot, right foot. Over centuries the bottom morphed into something resembling 少, but the walking is still there if you look.

歩く is walking as transportation — point A to point B on foot. 駅まで歩く (walk to the station). For aimless strolling, Japanese uses 散歩する (さんぽする) instead. Going somewhere: 歩く. Wandering: 散歩.

Particles: 歩く takes を to mark the path — 道を歩く (walk along the road). Route-marking を. で for location: 公園で歩く.

Passive に歩かれる: indirect passive. 隣の人に部屋の中を歩かれて眠れなかった (the neighbor walked around in their room and I couldn't sleep).

Causative を／に歩かせる: を coercive — 兵士を歩かせる (make the soldiers march). に permissive — 赤ちゃんに歩かせる (let the baby walk).

会う takes に, not を. You don't "meet" someone the way you'd "hit" a ball. Meeting is convergence — you move toward a person and arrive at the same point. 友達に会う. The に marks the person as a destination, the same way に works with 行く. Going to a place, going to a person — same particle, same logic.

The kanji 会: a lid (亼) over an opening. Things converging under one roof. The same kanji powers 会議 (かいぎ, meeting), 社会 (しゃかい, society), 会社 (かいしゃ, company).

会う vs 合う: Both read あう. 会う is people meeting. 合う is things matching or fitting. 友達に会う (meet a friend). サイズが合う (the size fits). Different kanji, related but distinct — two things coming together in different ways.

You can also use と to emphasize mutuality: 友達と会う (meet with a friend — both actively participating).

Particles: 会う takes に for the person met. 先生に会う. Use と for mutual meetings: 友達と会う.

Passive に会われる: rare in natural speech. The passive idea is usually expressed differently for this verb.

Causative を／に会わせる: making or letting someone meet. 二人を会わせる (introduce two people — make them meet).

空気を読む was lesson 91. Now meet the other half: 書く, the verb that turns sound into marks on a surface. Reading and writing — 読む and 書く — are the pair that defines literacy.

The kanji 書 layers a hand holding a brush (聿) over 曰 (to speak). A brush that speaks — writing as speech made visible. Even though you probably type now, 書く preserves the old connection between hand and language.

書く covers writing and drawing, though for serious art Japanese shifts to 描く (かく or えがく). 手紙を書く (write a letter). 名前を書く (write your name). In casual usage, 書く handles both.

Particles: 書く takes を for what you write. 小説を書く. Use に for the surface and で for the instrument: 紙に書く, ペンで書く.

Potential が書ける: が — what can be written becomes the subject. 漢字が書ける (I can write kanji).

Passive に書かれる: に marks the writer. 新聞に書かれた (it was written in the newspaper).

Causative に…を書かせる: に marks who you make write. 生徒に作文を書かせる (make students write an essay).

頭がいい — smart. 頭が悪い — stupid. Japanese doesn't dress it up with "bright" or "sharp" or "clever." You just rate the head directly. Good head, bad head. The equation of head with intelligence is universal, but Japanese wears it more openly than English.

The kanji 頭 has 頁 on the right — a component that originally depicted a human head. You'll see it in every head-and-face kanji: 顔 (かお, face), 額 (ひたい, forehead), 顎 (あご, chin). Spot 頁 on the right side of any kanji and it's probably about something on your head.

Three readings, three lives: あたま is the everyday word for your physical head. とう is a counter for large animals — 牛三頭 (three head of cattle). ず appears in compounds like 頭痛 (ずつう, headache). Same kanji, completely different contexts.

喧嘩を売る — to pick a fight. Literally: to sell a fight. You're putting a fight out there for someone to buy. And the response? 喧嘩を買う — buying the fight, accepting the challenge. The buy/sell metaphor maps perfectly onto provocation and response. You already know 買う (to buy) — now learn its other half.

売る is transitive — you sell something. 車を売る. 家を売る. Its intransitive partner 売れる (うれる) means something sells on its own: この本はよく売れている (this book sells well). 売る is your decision. 売れる is the market's response.

The kanji 売 has a display stand on top and legs walking below — something set up for people to come take away.

Particles: 売る takes を for what you sell. 本を売る. Add に for the buyer: 友達に車を売る (sell the car to a friend).

Potential が売れる: が — what can be sold becomes the subject. Note: 売れる also exists as the intransitive "to sell (well)," so context matters.

Passive に売られる: に marks the seller. 友達に売られた can mean "was sold by a friend" — or "was betrayed."

Causative に…を売らせる: に marks who you make sell. 店員に商品を売らせる (have the clerk sell the product).

大好きです, said to someone's face, is a love confession. This is the word that appears at the climax of every shoujo manga, every drama confession scene. Not 愛してる (あいしてる, I love you) — that exists but is almost too intense for daily use, the kind of thing married couples go years without saying. 大好き is the sweet spot: deep feeling without crushing weight.

The construction is transparent: 大 (big) + 好き (like) = big-like. Love. Japanese intensifies feelings by putting 大 in front: 大嫌い (だいきらい, hate), 大事 (だいじ, important).

For things, 大好き is enthusiastic: 猫が大好き (I love cats). ラーメンが大好き. Grammatically it works exactly like 好き — the thing loved takes が.

Dictionaryだいすきだ
Negativeだいすきじゃない
Pastだいすきだった
Past negだいすきじゃなかった
Adverbだいすきに
て-formだいすきで

体に気をつけて — take care of yourself. Literally: "pay attention to your body." This is how Japanese says goodbye when it means something — when you genuinely want the other person to stay well. The phrase reveals how Japanese thinks about health: through the body directly, not through abstract "feeling."

からだ is the body as a whole system. 体が強い (physically strong). 体の調子が悪い (feeling unwell — "the body's condition is bad"). You don't "feel sick" in Japanese. Your body's condition is off.

The kanji 体 is a simplified form of 體, which originally had 骨 (bone) — the body understood through its skeleton. The modern form uses 本 (root) next to the person radical: a person's fundamental physical self. In compounds, the reading shifts to たい: 体力 (たいりょく, stamina), 体重 (たいじゅう, body weight), 体温 (たいおん, body temperature).

すぐそこ — "right there." Not "soon there" — right there, very close, just a few steps away. Most learners meet すぐ as a time word (immediately, right away), but it works for space too: no gap in distance, no gap in time. A straight line from here to there, from now to then.

すぐ行く (I'll go right away). すぐ来る (coming immediately). 駅のすぐ近く (right near the station). The kanji 直 means straight, direct — a line without bends. すぐ applies that directness to both time and space.

You'll rarely see the kanji written. すぐ lives in hiragana. But 直 shows up in its other readings: 直す (なおす, to fix — make straight), 正直 (しょうじき, honest — correct and straight).

Look at 飛. Those two wing-like strokes fanning out on the right, the compact body on the left. It's a bird in flight, caught in a single character. Even after three thousand years of simplification from its oracle bone form — where it was obviously a bird with outspread wings — the flight is still visible. Few kanji are this expressive.

飛ぶ means both "to fly" and "to jump" — any movement that leaves the ground. 鳥が飛ぶ (a bird flies). ページが飛ぶ (a page is skipped — it "flies" over). 噂が飛ぶ (rumors spread). The common thread: something leaves its position and moves through space rapidly.

Particles: 飛ぶ takes を for the space traveled through — 空を飛ぶ (fly through the sky). Route-marking を. For destination: に or へ.

Passive に飛ばれる: rare. 鳥に飛ばれた (the bird flew away on me).

Causative: The transitive verb 飛ばす (とばす, to make fly, to skip) is used more naturally than the causative form 飛ばせる.

とてもできない — "I absolutely can't do it." Wait, doesn't とても mean "very"? It does — with positive statements. But pair it with a negative and it flips: "by no means," "not at all possible." とても間に合わない (there's no way we'll make it in time). This was actually the word's original meaning — "in any case, no matter what" — and the "very" sense came later.

In its everyday role, とても is the neutral, all-purpose intensifier. とてもおいしい (very delicious). とても大きい (very big). It sits before whatever it modifies. No complications.

とても vs すごく vs 非常に: all mean "very" at different registers. とても is neutral — works everywhere. すごく is casual, emphatic — friends gushing about food. 非常に (ひじょうに) is formal — news reports and papers. とても is the safe pick.

Japanese question words are shapeshifters. You learned this with いつ (when → always/someday/anytime). だれ does the same thing:

だれも → everyone (+ positive) / nobody (+ negative)
だれか → someone
だれでも → anyone

The も version is the tricky one. It flips based on the verb: だれもいる (everyone is here) vs だれもいない (nobody is here). Same word, opposite meanings, controlled entirely by positive or negative. It catches people off guard but follows the same logic as English "anybody."

The kanji 誰 has 言 (speech) on the left — a voice calling out, asking someone to identify themselves. The polite form is どなた: どなたですか (who might you be?) vs だれ？ (who?). The register gap is significant.

猫が好き. Not 猫を好き. The cats are the subject — they're the likeable ones. You aren't even grammatically present. This is one of the most important structural differences between English and Japanese: liking isn't framed as an action you perform. It's a quality of the thing liked. Cats are likeable. Coffee is likeable. Music is likeable. And you're the person those qualities happen to.

The kanji 好: 女 (woman) and 子 (child). A woman with a child — the image of natural affection, something precious held close.

好き covers everything from mild preference to romantic confession. コーヒーが好き (I like coffee). あの人が好き (I'm attracted to that person). In a confession, 好きです alone carries the full weight of "I have feelings for you." The simplicity is what makes it powerful.

Dictionaryすきだ
Negativeすきじゃない
Pastすきだった
Past negすきじゃなかった
Adverbすきに
て-formすきで

空気を読む — read the room. Literally: "read the air." The ability to sense unspoken mood and adjust your behavior is considered a fundamental social skill in Japan. Someone who can't is called KY (ケーワイ, short for 空気読めない). The verb went from reading text to reading people, and that jump tells you something about what Japanese culture values.

The kanji 読 has 言 (words) on the left. Reading is receiving words. The original meaning was reading aloud — in ancient China, reading was vocal, not silent. That older sense survives in 句読点 (くとうてん, punctuation).

本を読む. 新聞を読む. 漢字が読める (I can read kanji — one of the most satisfying sentences you'll eventually say).

Particles: 読む takes を — the text is your direct object. 本を読む.

Potential が読める: が marks what can be read. 漢字が読める.

Passive に読まれる: に marks the reader. 日記を母に読まれた (my diary was read by my mother) — classic adversative passive.

Causative に…を読ませる: に marks who you make read. 生徒に本を読ませる (have students read a book).

Politeよみますを

て-formよんでを

Causativeよませるに／を
Caus-passよませられるに

Imperativeよめを

Cond ～たらよんだらを

次は何？ — "What's next?" A tiny word you'll use a hundred times a day. 次の電車 (the next train). 次の日 (the next day). It always needs の when modifying a noun — 次電車 doesn't work. This marks it as a true noun, not an adjective.

In compounds, the reading shifts to じ and the meaning broadens to "order" or "secondary": 次第 (しだい, depends on / gradually), 目次 (もくじ, table of contents — literally "eye-order"). Recognize the reading switch: standalone つぎ, compound じ.

Learn this word. Then learn to almost never use it.

あなた is the textbook "you," and it's technically correct. But saying it to someone's face can sound cold, confrontational, or condescending. Japanese avoids second-person pronouns the way English avoids calling your boss by the wrong name. Use their name instead: 田中さんはどう思いますか. Or just drop the subject: どう思う？

The exceptions are specific. A wife calling her husband あなた is an established term of endearment — warm, domestic, like "dear." In surveys and instructions, あなた works because there's no specific person to name. In song lyrics, it appears constantly as a poetic, longing "you."

The kanji 貴方 — "precious direction" — reveals just how honorific this word once was. Centuries of wear ground it down.

You "drink" pills in Japanese. 薬を飲む (take medicine) — not "eat," not "take," but drink. If it goes down your throat, it's 飲む. This catches every English speaker off guard the first time.

Beyond medicine, 飲む is the standard verb for all drinking: 水を飲む, お茶を飲む, ビールを飲む. The kanji 飲 has the food radical on the left and 欠 (a person with mouth open) on the right. An open mouth near the food radical — but specifically for liquids.

お酒が飲める (I can drink alcohol) is how you say you drink — not as a question about what you're doing right now, but about capability and willingness. 飲める？ directed at someone is a common shorthand for "do you drink?"

Particles: 飲む takes を — the liquid is your direct object. ジュースを飲む.

Potential が飲める: が marks what can be drunk. この水が飲める (this water is drinkable).

Passive に飲まれる: に marks the drinker. 最後の一本を弟に飲まれた (my little brother drank the last one on me).

Causative に…を飲ませる: に marks who you make drink. 子供に薬を飲ませる (make the child take medicine).

五段 Godan他動詞

Never call a person 古い. The word is for things only — old buildings, old books, old traditions. An old person? Completely different vocabulary. Calling someone 古い means they're outdated, behind the times. その考え方は古い (that way of thinking is old-fashioned). It's a criticism, not a description of age.

With things, 古い pairs with 新しい (あたらしい, new). And it can carry warmth: 古い町並み (old townscape) evokes charm. 古い友達 (old friend) means a friend you've known long — not a friend who's aging.

The kanji 古: 十 (ten) over 口 (mouth). Ten generations of mouths passing down stories. That which has been spoken of for ages.

Dictionaryふるい
Negativeふるくない
Pastふるかった
Past negふるくなかった
Adverbふるく
て-formふるくて

質問がある — "I have a question." This is how you raise your hand in class, interrupt a presentation, or formally request information. 質問 is a deliberate, structured question — not idle wondering, not a quick aside.

質問する vs 聞く (きく, to ask): 聞く is casual, woven into conversation. 質問する is raising your hand. ちょっと聞いていい？ (can I ask something?) — friends talking. 質問してもいいですか (may I ask a question?) — meeting etiquette.

The kanji 問 is a mouth (口) inside a gate (門) — a voice at the gate, calling out to ask for entry. It shows up in 問題 (もんだい, problem), 疑問 (ぎもん, doubt), 訪問 (ほうもん, visit).

今 (now) + 日 (day) = today. The logic is transparent. The reading isn't: きょう has nothing to do with いま + ひ. It's a native Japanese word mapped onto Chinese characters. The regular compound reading こんにち also exists — you hear it daily in こんにちは, which literally means "as for this day."

きょう is a time word, so it needs no particle: 今日は暑い (it's hot today). 今日、学校に行く (I'm going to school today). No に required.

The time trio — 昨日 (きのう), 今日 (きょう), 明日 (あした) — all have irregular readings. The kanji say one thing, the pronunciation says another. These are among the oldest, most-used words in the language, and ancient native readings stuck even after kanji arrived. Learn them as units.

You don't "make" friends in Japanese. Friends happen. 友達ができた — literally, "a friend came into existence." できる means something formed, emerged, materialized. Japanese frames friendship as something that develops naturally, not something you manufacture. The grammar encodes a different philosophy of human connection.

The kanji 友 is two hands reaching in the same direction — people working together, aligned in purpose. In oracle bone script, it was two right hands side by side. The kanji 達 means "to reach, to arrive." Together: your fellow-reachers, the people alongside you.

友達 is the standard, default word for friend. 友達に会う (meet a friend). The word neighborhood: 親友 (しんゆう, close friend), 知り合い (しりあい, acquaintance), 仲間 (なかま, comrade). Japanese people can be particular about who qualifies as 友達 vs merely 知り合い.

Two different kanji, same pronunciation, overlapping meanings — the classic はやい trap. 早い is about timing: early, soon, premature. 速い is about speed: rapid, swift. 朝が早い (morning is early). 車が速い (the car is fast). In casual writing, the distinction blurs and 早い covers both. But when precision matters, the split is real.

The kanji 早: a sun (日) rising above a horizon. First light. Early morning. The jump from "early" to "fast" is intuitive — what arrives early moved quickly.

The compound 早速 (さっそく, immediately) combines both kanji — early + speed — and sounds decisive in conversation: 早速始めましょう (let's get started right away).

Dictionaryはやい
Negativeはやくない
Pastはやかった
Past negはやくなかった
Adverbはやく
て-formはやくて

どれ is "which one?" — but only when there are three or more options. For two options, use どっち (casual) or どちら (polite). This two-vs-three split doesn't exist in English and it trips people up early. コーヒーとお茶、どっち？ (coffee or tea?) — two choices. どれがいい？ (which one do you want?) — three or more laid out.

どれ is the question member of the ko-so-a-do thing series: これ (this), それ (that near you), あれ (that over there), どれ (which?). Before a noun, use どの instead: どの色？ (which color?).

Shapeshifting with particles, same as always: どれも (all of them / none + negative), どれか (one of them), どれでも (any of them).
